lesson.name: Anatomy and Physiology — Multi-Edition - Lesson 3 — Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy Atlas

tasks.chapterTitle: Skeletal, Nervous, Digestive, Muscular, and Urogenital Systems

messages.content:
### Lesson Summary: Atlas of Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy

The *Atlas of Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy* is an open-access, photographic educational resource. It originated as a 2017 student project at SUNY Oneonta to address the need for freely available anatomy study materials.

**Key Features:**
*   **Collaborative Content:** The majority of the atlas was created by students, covering dissections, photography, and anatomical identification [S.1].
*   **Purpose:** Intended as a supplementary tool to assist students in studying anatomy outside of the laboratory environment [S.1].
*   **Status:** The project remains a "work in progress" [S.1].

**Structural Overview:**
The atlas is organized into seven primary systems and specific focus areas:
*   **Organismal focus:** Lamprey anatomy [S.2].
*   **Systemic focus:**
    *   Skeletal [S.2]
    *   Nervous [S.2]
    *   Digestive [S.3]
    *   Muscular [S.3]
    *   Urogenital [S.3]
    *   Cardiovascular [S.4]

Lesson 3 — Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy Atlas - Presentation4 slides
Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy Atlas
  • A student-led project from SUNY Oneonta.
  • Provides accessible photographic resources for anatomy students.

The *Atlas of Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy* is an evolving, open-access resource originally developed in 2017 as a student project at SUNY Oneonta (p. 1). It was created to address the need for a comprehensive photographic guide for anatomy students, with content primarily contributed by students through dissection, photography, and specimen identification (p. 1). 

The atlas serves as an auxiliary study tool for use outside of the laboratory environment and currently features sections covering the following areas (p. 1, 2):

- Lamprey morphology 
- Skeletal systems
- Nervous systems

This document outlines the anatomical components covered in the Atlas of Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy. The key subjects are categorized as follows:

Core Anatomical Systems (p. 2-3)

- Biological Models: The text focuses on the study of the Lamprey (p. 2).
- Structural Systems: Coverage includes the organization of skeletal systems (p. 2) and muscular systems (p. 3).
- Physiological Systems: The text details the nervous systems (p. 2), digestive systems (p. 3), and urogenital systems (p. 3).

### Summary of Section S.4: Cardiovascular Systems

**Overview**
This section serves as a reference point within the "Atlas of Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy." It focuses on the cardiovascular systems of vertebrates, using the domestic cat as a primary subject for anatomical study.

**Key Points**
*   **Subject Matter:** The section provides anatomical documentation of the cardiovascular system, which is essential for understanding systemic blood flow and structural biology in vertebrates.
*   **Reference Material:** It includes visual resources, specifically highlighting a skeletal representation of a domestic cat to provide context for internal organ positioning.
*   **Source Context:** The content originates from the LibreTexts library, which maintains open-access educational resources for anatomy and physiology.

**Technical Note**
The study of cardiovascular dynamics often requires the evaluation of pressure gradients, typically represented by physical laws such as:
$$P = Q \times R$$
where $$P$$ represents pressure, $$Q$$ represents flow rate, and $$R$$ represents vascular resistance.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Why was the Atlas of Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy originally created?
options: To serve as a required textbook for SUNY Oneonta biology majors.
To address the absence of an accessible, comprehensive, photographic resource for anatomy students.
To provide a digital database for professional researchers to catalog new vertebrate species.
To replace the need for physical dissection labs in collegiate comparative anatomy courses.
questions.explanation: The atlas was initiated as a project specifically because there was a lack of a comprehensive, freely-accessible photographic atlas available to students.

questions.question: Based on the development of the atlas, what is the primary source of its content?
options: Curated images provided by veterinary medical journals.
Professional photographic contributions from wildlife photographers.
Labor performed by students including dissection, photography, and identification.
Public domain historical illustrations sourced exclusively from the 19th century.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the majority of the entries in the atlas were produced by students, covering the work of dissection, photography, and identification.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the current status and intended use of the atlas?
options: It is a finished, static reference guide used strictly for official laboratory examinations.
It is an ongoing, evolving project designed to supplement laboratory study.
It is an exclusive internal database meant only for SUNY Oneonta faculty use.
It is a discontinued project that now serves only as a historical archive of 2017 anatomical methods.
questions.explanation: The atlas is described as a 'work in progress' that aims to be a tool for students to study anatomy outside of the laboratory setting.

questions.question: If a student is studying the location of the kidneys and reproductive organs across different vertebrates, which section of the atlas would be most relevant?
options: Section 3: Nervous systems
Section 5: Muscular systems
Section 6: Urogenital systems
Section 7: Cardiovascular systems
questions.explanation: Urogenital systems is the biological term used to categorize both the urinary (kidneys) and reproductive organs, making it the correct section for this study.

questions.question: The inclusion of the domestic cat skeleton in the atlas highlights which characteristic of the project?
options: A focus on mammalian diagnostic medicine rather than vertebrate evolution.
The use of varied resources, including public domain materials, to supplement student work.
The limitation of the atlas to primarily domestic animals.
The requirement that all specimens must be photographed in a laboratory setting exclusively.
questions.explanation: The mention of the public domain skeleton of a domestic cat indicates that the project incorporates diverse resources alongside student-created content to provide comprehensive coverage.

cards.front: What is the origin of the Atlas of Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy?
cards.back: It began as a student class project at SUNY Oneonta in 2017.

cards.front: Why was the Atlas of Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy created?
cards.back: Due to the lack of a comprehensive, freely-accessible photographic atlas for anatomy students.

cards.front: Who produced the majority of the content, including dissection and photography, for this atlas?
List the seven main anatomical systems featured in the atlas.
cards.back: Lamprey, skeletal, nervous, digestive, muscular, urogenital, and cardiovascular systems.

cards.front: What is the primary purpose of the atlas for students?
cards.back: To serve as a useful tool for studying anatomy outside of the laboratory.

cards.front: Is the Atlas of Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy considered a completed, static work?
cards.back: No, it is described as a work in progress.

cards.front: What animal's skeleton is featured in the atlas thumbnail?
Who provided the public domain image of the domestic cat skeleton?
Which specific organism is listed as the first entry in the atlas content structure?
What is the accessibility status of the Atlas of Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy?

slides.title: Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy Atlas
bullets: A student-led project from SUNY Oneonta.
Provides accessible photographic resources for anatomy students.
slides.notes: This atlas was developed to support anatomy students with a comprehensive, freely available visual guide for laboratory studies.

slides.title: Lamprey and Core Systems
bullets: Lamprey anatomy serves as an entry point.
Fundamental skeletal and nervous system documentation.
slides.notes: We begin our study with the lamprey, moving into the essential foundation of skeletal and nervous structures.

slides.title: Internal Organ Systems
bullets: Atlas entries on various muscular systems.
slides.notes: Continuing our survey, we examine the digestive, muscular, and urogenital systems that maintain bodily functions.

slides.title: Cardiovascular Anatomy
slides.notes: Finally, we conclude the atlas with the essential cardiovascular system and supporting reference materials.
